questionText: Find the range of values of $k$ for which $x^2 + 6x + k > 0$ for all real values of $x$.

steps.explanation: For $x^2 + 6x + k > 0$ to hold for every $x$, the upward parabola must never touch or cross the $x$-axis.

steps.description: State the condition for no real roots.
steps.explanation: An upward parabola stays strictly above the axis exactly when the quadratic has no real roots, i.e. a negative discriminant.
Here the constant term is the unknown $k$, which the condition will constrain.
For no real roots the discriminant must be strictly negative.
Adding $4k$ to both sides moves it across.
Dividing both sides by the positive number $4$ preserves the inequality.
So the condition holds for $k$ greater than $9$.
At $k = 10$ the discriminant is negative, so the quadratic is always positive — confirming the range.
At $k = 9$ the discriminant is zero, so the parabola just touches the axis and $> 0$ fails at one point; hence $9$ is excluded.
At $k = 9$ the expression is $(x+3)^2$, which is zero at $x = -3$, so it is not strictly positive — consistent with excluding $9$.
Only for $k > 9$ is the quadratic strictly positive for every real $x$.
The quadratic is positive for all real $x$ when $k > 9$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $\Delta = 36 - 4k < 0 \Rightarrow k > 9$ (so the parabola never reaches the $x$-axis).
